ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 0, 47, 55, 770207, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL
- `telefonnummer` text NULL
- `position` text NULL
- `funktion` text NULL
- `sprache` text NULL
- `kunde` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets  (400 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `seriennr` text NULL
- `garantie_bis` text NULL
- `kd_ref` text NULL
- `projekt_ref` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen  (235 rows)

Columns:
- `chance_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `phase` text NULL
- `abschlussdatum` text NULL
- `volumen` double precision NULL
- `waehrung` text NULL
- `kd_nr` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden  (100 rows)

Columns:
- `kunden_nr` text NOT NULL
- `firmenname` text NULL
- `erp_nummer` text NULL
- `kategorie` text NULL
- `gebiet` text NULL
- `branche` text NULL
- `webseite` text NULL
- `ort` text NULL
- `land` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj  (155 rows)

Columns:
- `proj_id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `kd` text NULL
- `opp` text NULL

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 0, 47, 55, 770217, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 0, 47, 55, 770523, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f497e-d178-7366-8a3c-5eb4b9a408fc', conversation_id='019f497e-d178-7366-8a3c-5eb32a789567')

   - `StageName`: `phase` -> Enum (Prospecting, ...)
   - `CloseDate`: `abschlussdatum` -> Date parsing (DD.MM.YYYY or similar)

   - `Name`: `name`

  - *Actually*, in many of these "fixture" problems, the target `Id` might just be the source ID if it's already an external system ID, OR the problem expects me to flag the mismatch. I will explicitly map: Source Key -> Legacy Column. Target FK -> Transformed Key (assuming a function or join is needed).

- **Enums**:
  - `Customer_Tier__c` (`kategorie`): Map German terms (Gold/Silber/Bronze/Platin) to English? Or keep as is if they match the target enum values provided. Target enum: Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum. Source likely German ("Silber", "Platin"). Map required.

  - `Preferred_Language__c` (`sprache`): Target (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT). Source might be full words or codes. Normalize to ISO codes.
  - `StageName` (`phase`): Target English terms. Source likely German ("Akquise", "Qualifizierung"). Map required.
  - `Project_Status__c` (`status`): Target (Active, Completed...). Source likely German ("Aktiv", "Erledigt"). Map required.

- **Dates**:

- **Amounts**:
  - `volumen` is double precision source. But rule says "handle European separators". Even if numeric, it's safer to treat as text, remove dots (thousands), replace comma with dot, then cast, in case it comes in as text with formatting later. Since source type is already double precision, standard casting should suffice unless locale issues are introduced by the toolchain. I will stick to safe string manipulation logic: `REGEXP_REPLACE(CAST(...))`.

**Detailed Table Plans:**

### 1. Account
- **Source**: `kunden`

data.overview:
## Global Mapping Strategy

### Key Resolution (Critical)
All source tables use German keys (`kunden_nr`, `chance_id`, `proj_id`, `asset_id`). Target schema uses Salesforce-style IDs. 
- **Transformation**: Apply a deterministic transform to map source keys to `Id` columns in target tables. 
  - Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix (e.g., `REPLACE(kunden_nr, 'K', '001')`).
  - This transformed key must be used consistently for all `AccountId`, `Account__c`, and related FK columns.
- **Legacy Columns**: Populate `Legacy_*__c` with the raw source keys (`ap_id`, `kunden_nr`, etc.) for traceability.

### Enum & Data Normalization
- **Enums**: Map German source terms to English target enums (e.g., 'Silber' -> 'Silver', 'Akquise' -> 'Prospecting'). Default to NULL if no match found.
- **Languages**: Normalize `sprache` column to ISO codes (DE, EN, etc.).

### Date Handling
- All date columns (`abschlussdatum`, `garantie_bis`, `go_live`) should be parsed using `TO_DATE(col, 'DD.MM.YYYY')`. If format is non-standard, use regex guards. Output as ISO text `YYYY-MM-DD` or NULL if empty/unparseable.

### Amounts
- Source `volumen` is numeric, but apply standard European cleaning (`REGEXP_REPLACE` to strip '.', swap ',' for '.') before casting to Double Precision to ensure locale safety.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 0, 58, 23, 969273, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Account`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: ## Global Mapping Strategy

### Key Resolution (Critical)
All source tables use German keys (`kunden_nr`, `chance_id`, `proj_id`, `asset_id`). Target schema uses Salesforce-style IDs. 
- **Transformation**: Apply a deterministic transform to map source keys to `Id` columns in target tables. 
  - Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix (e.g., `REPLACE(kunden_nr, 'K', '001')`).
  - This transformed key must be used consistently for all `AccountId`, `Account__c`, and related FK columns.
- **Legacy Columns**: Populate `Legacy_*__c` with the raw source keys (`ap_id`, `kunden_nr`, etc.) for traceability.

### Enum & Data Normalization
- **Enums**: Map German source terms to English target enums (e.g., 'Silber' -> 'Silver', 'Akquise' -> 'Prospecting'). Default to NULL if no match found.
- **Languages**: Normalize `sprache` column to ISO codes (DE, EN, etc.).

### Date Handling
- All date columns (`abschlussdatum`, `garantie_bis`, `go_live`) should be parsed using `TO_DATE(col, 'DD.MM.YYYY')`. If format is non-standard, use regex guards. Output as ISO text `YYYY-MM-DD` or NULL if empty/unparseable.

### Amounts

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The user wants a dbt model for the `Account` target table based on the `kunde` source table (`fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden`).

Let's review the source and target columns:
Source: `kunden_nr`, `firmenname`, `erp_nummer`, `kategorie`, `gebiet`, `branche`, `webseite`, `ort`, `land`.
Target: `Id`, `Name`, `ERP_Number__c`, `Customer_Tier__c`, `Region__c`, `Industry`, `Website`, `BillingCity`, `BillingCountry`, `Legacy_Customer_ID__c`, `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted`.

Transformations according to the plan:
- `Id`: `REPLACE(kunden_nr, 'K', '001')` (assuming source uses 'K' prefix)
- `Name`: `INITCAP(firmenname)`
- `ERP_Number__c`: `TRIM(erp_nummer)`
- `Customer_Tier__c`: Map German tier names to English. E.g., 'Gold' -> 'Gold', 'Silber' -> 'Silver', 'Bronze' -> 'Bronze', 'Platin' -> 'Platinum'. Default NULL.
- `Region__c`: `TRIM(gebiet)`
- `Industry`: `INITCAP(branche)`
- `Website`: `webseite`
- `BillingCity`: `INITCAP(ort)`
- `BillingCountry`: `UPPER(land)` or map to ISO code. I'll stick to `UPPER(TRIM(land))` for simplicity, or just pass it through as specified: `UPPER(land)`. The plan says "If full country name provided, map to ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code." Since I don't have the actual data, I'll keep it simple but maybe add a CASE for common ones, or just `INITCAP(land)` as per standard, but plan says `UPPER(land)`. I'll stick to `INITCAP(TRIM(land))` or just `TRIM(land)` and let `UPPER` be implicit if needed, but I'll follow the prompt's explicit mapping: `UPPER(TRIM(land))`. Actually, I'll just use `INITCAP(TRIM(land))` to be safe, or just `TRIM(land)`. The plan says `BillingCountry <- land (UPPER(land). If full country name provided, map to ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code.)`
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c`: `TRIM(kunden_nr)`
- `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`: NULL (or static load date)

Wait, the prompt says: "Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments."
And: "Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model."
"Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}`..."

Let's construct the SQL carefully.

```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    REPLACE("kunden_nr", 'K', '001') AS "Id",
    INITCAP("firmenname") AS "Name",
    TRIM("erp_nummer") AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM("kategorie")) = 'GOLD' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM("kategorie")) = 'SILBER' THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM("kategorie")) = 'BRONZE' THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM("kategorie")) = 'PLATIN' THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM("gebiet") AS "Region__c",
    INITCAP("branche") AS "Industry",
    "webseite" AS "Website",
    INITCAP(TRIM("ort")) AS "BillingCity",
    UPPER(TRIM("land")) AS "BillingCountry",
    TRIM("kunden_nr") AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'kunden') }}
```

Check against target schema:
- `Id` text NOT NULL -> REPLACE handles it. If kunden_nr has no K, it might fail length check if expected to be 15 chars? The plan says `REPLACE(kunden_nr, 'K', '001')`. I'll follow exactly.
- `Name` text NOT NULL -> INITCAP(firmenname). If null, maybe COALESCE? Plan says `INITCAP(firmenname)`. I'll stick to it.
- `ERP_Number__c` text -> TRIM(erp_nummer)
- `Customer_Tier__c` enum in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum) -> CASE mapping
- `Region__c` text -> TRIM(gebiet)
- `Industry` text -> INITCAP(branche)
- `Website` text -> webseite
- `BillingCity` text -> INITCAP(ort)
- `BillingCountry` text -> UPPER(land)
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text -> TRIM(kunden_nr)
- `CreatedDate` text -> NULL

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 0, 59, 37, 531381, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

# Target schema

- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: ## Global Mapping Strategy

### Key Resolution (Critical)
All source tables use German keys (`kunden_nr`, `chance_id`, `proj_id`, `asset_id`). Target schema uses Salesforce-style IDs. 
- **Transformation**: Apply a deterministic transform to map source keys to `Id` columns in target tables. 
  - Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix (e.g., `REPLACE(kunden_nr, 'K', '001')`).
  - This transformed key must be used consistently for all `AccountId`, `Account__c`, and related FK columns.
- **Legacy Columns**: Populate `Legacy_*__c` with the raw source keys (`ap_id`, `kunden_nr`, etc.) for traceability.

### Enum & Data Normalization
- **Enums**: Map German source terms to English target enums (e.g., 'Silber' -> 'Silver', 'Akquise' -> 'Prospecting'). Default to NULL if no match found.
- **Languages**: Normalize `sprache` column to ISO codes (DE, EN, etc.).

### Date Handling
- All date columns (`abschlussdatum`, `garantie_bis`, `go_live`) should be parsed using `TO_DATE(col, 'DD.MM.YYYY')`. If format is non-standard, use regex guards. Output as ISO text `YYYY-MM-DD` or NULL if empty/unparseable.

### Amounts
- Source `volumen` is numeric, but apply standard European cleaning (`REGEXP_REPLACE` to strip '.', swap ',' for '.') before casting to Double Precision to ensure locale safety.

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The user wants a dbt model for the `Contact` target table based on source tables `ansprechpartner` and `kunden`.

Let's analyze the required columns for Contact:
- `Id` text NOT NULL -> Mapped from `ap_id`. The plan says to apply a deterministic transform. For accounts, it says replace 'K' with '001'. But wait, `ap_id` might start with something else. I'll just cast `ap_id` to text, or maybe format it as `003` (SF Contact prefix) + REPLACE? Actually, the plan says "Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix". It doesn't specify Contact ID format exactly. I'll just use `ap_id` directly for `Id` since I don't have sample data to infer the exact prefix transformation, or I'll just output `TRIM(ap_id)`. Wait, Salesforce Contact IDs typically start with '003'. But without explicit instruction, I'll just cast `ap_id` to text and maybe replace a known prefix if present. Actually, the prompt says "Populate every Legacy_*__c column from the source natural key". So `Id` needs a transform. I'll just use `REPLACE(ap_id, 'A', '003')` or simply `ap_id`. To be safe, I'll just output `ap_id::text AS "Id"` and keep it simple, or follow the pattern: maybe replace 'AP' with '003'? The prompt doesn't give sample rows. I'll stick to `TRIM(ap_id)` for `Id` but since it says NOT NULL, I'll ensure it's cast to text. Actually, Salesforce IDs are 15/18 chars. I'll just output `ap_id` as is, or apply a simple replace if needed. I'll just use `CAST(TRIM(ap_id) AS TEXT)`.
Wait, the prompt says: "AccountId and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ." This implies other tables might just use raw keys or a simple transform. I'll use `ap_id` as is for Contact's `Id`.

Let's look at the mapping plan provided:
- Legacy_Contact_ID__c <- ap_id (TRIM(ap_id))
- FirstName <- vorname (INITCAP(vorname))
- LastName <- nachname (INITCAP(nachname))
- Notes: Join Account via transformed keys. `nachname` is NOT NULL in target; apply COALESCE(nachname, 'Unknown').

Other Contact columns:
- `Email` <- `email_adresse`
- `Phone` <- `telefonnummer`
- `Title` <- `position` or `funktion`? Usually `position` is Job Title. I'll use `position`.
- `Role__c` <- enum in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor). Source doesn't have a direct role column mentioned in the plan. Maybe `funktion`? Or NULL fallback. I'll map `funktion` if it matches, else NULL.
- `Preferred_Language__c` <- `sprache`. Map to DE, EN, FR, ES, IT. German names usually: 'Deutsch' -> 'DE', 'Englisch' -> 'EN', etc. Or maybe source is already ISO codes? I'll assume we might need INITCAP or direct match, fallback NULL.
- `AccountId` <- needs to be the transformed Account Id from `kunden.kunden_nr`. The plan says replace 'K' with '001'. So `REPLACE(kunden.kunden_nr, 'K', '001')`. I will apply this transform to join and output.
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` <- `ap_id`
- `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` <- Source table doesn't have these. I'll set them to NULL or default values as they are typically system-generated, but dbt models often just leave them NULL or use current timestamp if needed. The prompt says "Target schema ... CreatedDate text, LastModifiedDate text, IsDeleted integer". I'll output NULL for dates and 0 for IsDeleted, or just omit them? No, must match target spec. I'll use NULL for dates, 0 for IsDeleted. Actually, in these mappings, if not in source, we usually put NULL or a default.

Let's construct the SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    CAST(TRIM(ap.ap_id) AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(ap.vorname)) AS "FirstName",
    COALESCE(INITCAP(TRIM(ap.nachname)), 'Unknown') AS "LastName",
    TRIM(ap.email_adresse) AS "Email",
    TRIM(ap.telefonnummer) AS "Phone",
    INITCAP(TRIM(ap.position)) AS "Title",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(ap.funktion)) IN ('ENTSCHEIDER', 'DECISION MAKER') THEN 'Decision Maker'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(ap.funktion)) IN ('ENDBENUTZER', 'END USER') THEN 'End User'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(ap.funktion)) IN ('TECHNISCHER KONTAKT', 'TECHNICAL CONTACT') THEN 'Technical Contact'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(ap.funktion)) IN ('EXECUTIVE SPONSOR', 'VORSITZENDER SPONSOR') THEN 'Executive Sponsor'
        ELSE NULL

    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(ap.sprache)) LIKE '%DE%' OR TRIM(ap.sprache) = 'Deutsch' THEN 'DE'
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 1, 1, 31, 767237, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The user is telling me that `query_postgres` is not available as a tool. I need to produce the final result directly without querying the database. Let me analyze what I have and create the dbt model for Opportunity.

Based on the source schema:
- `chancen` table has: chance_id, bezeichnung, phase, abschlussdatum, volumen, waehrung, kd_nr

The transformation plan says:
- Id: Replace 'K' prefix with '001' (but this is for Account - Opportunities likely need a different ID format, perhaps starting with '006')
- Actually, looking at Salesforce convention:
  - Accounts start with '001'
  - Contacts start with '005'  
  - Opportunities start with '006'
  - Projects would need their own prefix
  - Assets would need their own prefix

But wait, the mapping plan says "Apply a deterministic transform to map source keys to `Id` columns in target tables. Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix"

So for Opportunity, I need to think about what the proper transformation is. Since we're joining on kd_nr -> kunden.kunden_nr, and those use 'K' prefix being replaced by '001', then for Opportunity Ids (which come from chance_id), I should apply a similar pattern but with opportunity-specific prefix.

Let me look at what we know:
- source chancen.chance_id would be like "OP001" or something similar
- target Id should follow SF convention starting with '006'

Actually, let me just use a consistent transformation: if chance_id starts with 'O', replace it with '006'. If there's no prefix to replace, prepend '006'.

For StageName mapping from German phases (need to map common German sales pipeline stages):
- Akquise/Neukundengewinnung -> Prospecting


- Qualifizierung -> Qualification
- Bedarfsanalyse -> Needs Analysis
- Wert proposition/Angebotserstellung -> Value Proposition
- Entscheidungsträger identifizieren -> Id. Decision Makers
- Wahrnehmungsanalyse/Analysephase -> Perception Analysis
- Angebot/Preisangebot -> Proposal/Price Quote
- Verhandlung/Klausur -> Negotiation/Review
- Abschluss erfolgreich/Abschluss gewonnen -> Closed Won
- Abschluss fehlerhaft/Abschluss verloren -> Closed Lost

Now I'm mapping the remaining German phases to their English equivalents and preparing to write the SQL transformation logic. I need to complete the partial thought about the Id transformation and then summarize the approach for the Opportunity model.

The Id follows a pattern similar to Account: replacing source prefixes with Salesforce-style Ids. Since Accounts use "K" -> "001", Opportunities likely use "O" -> "006" based on standard SFDC conventions. The Chance_ID column transforms accordingly, mapping each prefix to its corresponding target format.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 1, 2, 52, 711939, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Project__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

# Target schema

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:

- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: ## Global Mapping Strategy

### Key Resolution (Critical)
All source tables use German keys (`kunden_nr`, `chance_id`, `proj_id`, `asset_id`). Target schema uses Salesforce-style IDs. 
- **Transformation**: Apply a deterministic transform to map source keys to `Id` columns in target tables. 
  - Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix (e.g., `REPLACE(kunden_nr, 'K', '001')`).
  - This transformed key must be used consistently for all `AccountId`, `Account__c`, and related FK columns.
- **Legacy Columns**: Populate `Legacy_*__c` with the raw source keys (`ap_id`, `kunden_nr`, etc.) for traceability.

### Enum & Data Normalization
- **Enums**: Map German source terms to English target enums (e.g., 'Silber' -> 'Silver', 'Akquise' -> 'Prospecting'). Default to NULL if no match found.
- **Languages**: Normalize `sprache` column to ISO codes (DE, EN, etc.).

### Date Handling
- All date columns (`abschlussdatum`, `garantie_bis`, `go_live`) should be parsed using `TO_DATE(col, 'DD.MM.YYYY')`. If format is non-standard, use regex guards. Output as ISO text `YYYY-MM-DD` or NULL if empty/unparseable.

### Amounts
- Source `volumen` is numeric, but apply standard European cleaning (`REGEXP_REPLACE` to strip '.', swap ',' for '.') before casting to Double Precision to ensure locale safety.

Source tables: fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj, fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden, fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen
Join keys: proj.kd = kunden.kunden_nr, proj.opp = chancen.chance_id
- Legacy_Project_ID__c <- proj_id (TRIM(proj_id))
- Name <- name (name)
- Project_Status__c <- status (Map German project status (e.g., 'In Bearbeitung' -> 'Active') to target Enum.)
- Go_Live_Date__c <- go_live (Parse date format (likely DD.MM.YYYY). Cast to YYYY-MM-DD text. NULL if missing.)
Notes: Joins both Account and Opportunity. `proj.opp` maps to `chancen.chance_id`. Apply consistent Key Transform for `Account__c` (from `kd`) and `Opportunity__c` (from `opp`).

Follow the plan. Produce the dbt model for `Project__c`.", timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 1, 2, 52, 711948, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 1, 2, 52, 712299, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f498c-8126-734c-bf6c-b7b0c695e31c', conversation_id='019f498c-8126-734c-bf6c-b7af243f1301')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='The user wants a dbt model for `Project__c` table, transforming source data from `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src`.

Let me analyze the requirements:
- Target: `Project__c` with columns Id, Name, Project_Status__c, Go_Live_Date__c, Account__c, Opportunity__c, Legacy_Project_ID__c, CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted
- Source tables needed: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj`, `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden`, `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen`
- Join keys: proj.kd = kunden.kunden_nr, proj.opp = chancen.chance_id

Transformation rules:
1. Key resolution: Source uses German keys with 'K' prefix (kunden_nr), need to transform to Salesforce-style IDs with '001' prefix for Account__c. Similarly for Opportunity, replace 'C' or similar prefix with appropriate SF ID prefix (probably '006' for opportunities).

Wait, let me check the approved mapping plan:
- Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix (e.g., `REPLACE(kunden_nr, 'K', '001')`)
- This transformed key must be used consistently for all `AccountId`, `Account__c`, and related FK columns.

For Opportunity, the plan says "proj.opp maps to chancen.chance_id". I need to figure out what prefix chance_id uses. The column is named `chance_id` which suggests German "Chance" (opportunity). It might have a 'C' prefix or similar. Let me assume it follows the same pattern but with an opportunity-specific SF ID prefix. Typically Salesforce Opportunity IDs start with '006'. So if source has 'C12345', it would become '006...'.

Actually, looking at the guidelines again: "Apply a deterministic transform to map source keys to `Id` columns in target tables. Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix". For opportunities, I should apply similar logic - replace the chance_id prefix with an opportunity-specific prefix. But since I don't know what prefix chance_id uses, I'll make a reasonable assumption based on common German business software (likely 'C' or just numbers). Let me assume it might start with 'C' and transform to '006'.

Wait, looking more carefully at the schema - `chancen` table has `chance_id`. Given the German naming convention and consistency with `kunden_nr`, it's likely prefixed with something like 'C'. But I should be careful. Let me assume:
- kunden.kunden_nr starts with 'K' → replace with '001' for Account__c
- chancen.chance_id starts with 'C' → replace with '006' for Opportunity__c

For the project table itself, proj.proj_id likely has its own prefix. I'll map it to the Project__c Id field and populate Legacy_Project_ID__c with the raw proj_id.

2. Dates: Parse using TO_DATE with DD.MM.YYYY format, output as YYYY-MM-DD text
3. Enums: Map German status values to English enum values for Project_Status__c

Let me write the SQL:

```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    -- Id: Transform proj_id to Salesforce-style Project ID (00H prefix)
    '00H' || REPLACE(TRIM(proj.proj_id), 'P', '') AS "Id",
    
    -- Name
    TRIM(proj.name) AS "Name",
    
    -- Project_Status__c: Map German status to English enum
    CASE INITCAP(TRIM(proj.status))
        WHEN 'In Bearbeitung' THEN 'Active'
        WHEN 'Abgeschlossen' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN 'In Planung' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN 'Auf Eisgelegt' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN 'Storniert' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    
    -- Go_Live_Date__c: Parse date from DD.MM.YYYY format
    CASE 
        WHEN TRIM(proj.go_live) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' 
        THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(proj.go_live), 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    
    -- Account__c: Transform kd to Salesforce Account ID (001 prefix)
    '001' || REPLACE(TRIM(kunden.kunden_nr), 'K', '') AS "Account__c",
    
    -- Opportunity__c: Transform opp to Salesforce Opportunity ID (006 prefix)
    '006' || REPLACE(TRIM(chancen.chance_id), 'C', '') AS "Opportunity__c",
    
    -- Legacy_Project_ID__c: Raw proj_id for traceability
    TRIM(proj.proj_id) AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    
    -- CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted: Source doesn't seem to have these directly
    -- I'll use NULL or some default since the source table 'proj' doesn't show these columns
    CAST(NULL AS TEXT) AS "CreatedDate",
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 1, 11, 1, 403196, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

# Target schema


## Account

- `Go_Live_Date__c` text

- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL

- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: ## Global Mapping Strategy

### Key Resolution (Critical)
All source tables use German keys (`kunden_nr`, `chance_id`, `proj_id`, `asset_id`). Target schema uses Salesforce-style IDs. 
- **Transformation**: Apply a deterministic transform to map source keys to `Id` columns in target tables. 
  - Example: Replace source 'K' prefix with SF '001' Account prefix (e.g., `REPLACE(kunden_nr, 'K', '001')`).
  - This transformed key must be used consistently for all `AccountId`, `Account__c`, and related FK columns.
- **Legacy Columns**: Populate `Legacy_*__c` with the raw source keys (`ap_id`, `kunden_nr`, etc.) for traceability.

### Enum & Data Normalization
- **Enums**: Map German source terms to English target enums (e.g., 'Silber' -> 'Silver', 'Akquise' -> 'Prospecting'). Default to NULL if no match found.
- **Languages**: Normalize `sprache` column to ISO codes (DE, EN, etc.).

### Date Handling
- All date columns (`abschlussdatum`, `garantie_bis`, `go_live`) should be parsed using `TO_DATE(col, 'DD.MM.YYYY')`. If format is non-standard, use regex guards. Output as ISO text `YYYY-MM-DD` or NULL if empty/unparseable.

### Amounts
- Source `volumen` is numeric, but apply standard European cleaning (`REGEXP_REPLACE` to strip '.', swap ',' for '.') before casting to Double Precision to ensure locale safety.

Source tables: fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets, fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden, fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj

- Legacy_Asset_ID__c <- asset_id (TRIM(asset_id))

- Serial_Number__c <- seriennr (seriennr)
- Warranty_End_Date__c <- garantie_bis (Parse date format (likely DD.MM.YYYY). Cast to YYYY-MM-DD text.)
Notes: Joins both Account and Project. `assets.projekt_ref` maps to `proj.proj_id`. Apply consistent Key Transform for FK columns.

Follow the plan. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 1, 16, 46, 533042, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql

SELECT

    CASE

        ELSE CONCAT('006', RIGHT(LPAD(ABS(MD5(chance_id)::BIGINT::TEXT), 10, '0'), 8))
    END AS "Id",

    -- Name: From bezeichnung, initcap for proper casing
    INITCAP(TRIM(bezeichnung)) AS "Name",

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('qualifikation', 'qualification', 'qualifizierung') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('bedarfsanalyse', 'needs analysis', 'bedarfsermittlung') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('wert proposition', 'value proposition', 'nutzen aufzeigen', 'konzepterstellung') THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('entscheidungsträger identifizieren', 'id. decision makers', 'multipler identification', 'key player analyse') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('wahrnehmungsanalyse', 'perception analysis', 'analysepipeline', 'vergleich') THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('angebot', 'preisangebot', 'proposal', 'price quote', 'angebotserstellung', 'vorschlag') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('verhandlung', 'negotiation', 'klausur', 'review', 'verhandlungsphase', 'konditionenbesprechung') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('abschluss erfolgreich', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'verkauf erfolgreich', 'successful closing') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(phase)) IN ('geschlossen verloren', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgelehnt', 'failed closing', 'nicht gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",

    -- CloseDate: Parse DD.MM.YYYY format, output ISO YYYY-MM-DD
    CASE
        WHEN abschlussdatum IS NULL OR TRIM(abschlussdatum) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN TRIM(abschlussdatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(abschlussdatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN TRIM(abschlussdatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN CONCAT(SUBSTR(TRIM(abschlussdatum), 5, 4), '-', SUBSTR(TRIM(abschlussdatum), 3, 2), '-', SUBSTR(TRIM(abschlussdatum), 1, 2))
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",

    -- Amount: Clean European format (strip symbols, remove thousand-sep dots, swap comma to period)
    CASE
        WHEN volumen IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(volumen, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g') ~ '^\d+(\.\d{3})*,\d+$' THEN
            CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(volumen, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g'), '\.', '') || REPLACE(SUBSTR(REGEXP_REPLACE(volumen, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g'), LENGTH(REGEXP_REPLACE(volumen, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g')) - POSITION(',' IN REGEXP_REPLACE(volumen, '[^\d.,]', '', 'g')) + 1), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN volumen ~ '^[-+]?\d+,\d+$' THEN CAST(REPLACE(volumen, ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN volumen ~ '^[-+]?\d+\.\d+$' OR volumen ~ '^[-+]?\d+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(volumen, '[^\d.-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Amount",

    -- CurrencyIsoCode: Normalize to ISO 4217 codes
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€', 'euros') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('usd', 'us dollar', 'us dollars', '$', 'dollars') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('gbp', 'pound sterling', '£', 'pounds', 'british pound') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('chf', 'swiss franc', 'swiss francs', 'francs') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrung)) IN ('cad', 'canadian dollar', 'canadian dollars') THEN 'CAD'
        WHEN waehrung ~ '^[A-Z]{3}$' THEN UPPER(TRIM(waehrung))
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",

    -- AccountId: Transform kd_nr (K prefix) to Salesforce Account Id (001 prefix) for FK
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(kd_nr) ~ '^K[0-9]+$' THEN REPLACE(LOWER(TRIM(kd_nr)), 'k', '001')
        ELSE CONCAT('001', RIGHT(LPAD(ABS(MD5(kd_nr)::BIGINT::TEXT), 10, '0'), 8))
    END AS "AccountId",

    -- Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c: Raw source chance_id
    TRIM(chance_id) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",

    -- Audit columns (defaults since not in source)
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }};
```
